Sentences: Mi bin gwine da mauma house yestadee, but rain come down haad.
She da cook rice now while de chillun da set de table.
We gwine fish een de creek tomorruh ef de tide stay low.
Ef oona bin call me early, mi woulda bring de basket.
De gate done lock befo de mule get loose.
Ain nobody see de boat wen fog cover de ribba.
Dem house bin buil by de men from Johns Island.
He beena wuk een de field since sun come up.
Tek yo time so de pot ain spill on de flo.
By next week, we gwine done fix de roof and paint de step.
How oona do dis maanin? We da do good, tankee, how you?
Wa happen at de market? De shrimp sell out befo noon.
You gwine church Sunday? Yes, ef Granny feel strong nuff.
Mi hear music by de wharf. Dat must be dem boys wid de drum.
Come yah lil mo. I got someting sweet fo tell you.
Why you laugh so? Dat story mek mi belly hurt.
De bus late again? E tru, but we still reach town on time.
You want tea or lemonade? Gimme lemonade, but ain put too much sugar.
Mi miss you plenty. Mi miss you too, fa true.
Oona ready fo go? Hol on, mi still lookin fo mi hat.
Mi boss say de meeting start at nine, so oona mus be on time.
She order coffee, but she tell de lady, mek um sweet lak home.
De teacher ask fo homework, and de class answer, we done submit um online.
Mi phone battery dead, so mi cyan check de message right now.
Dem chillun da practice Spanish words, but dey still talk Gullah at recess.
He say de Wi-Fi slow, but de old radio wuk jus fine.
Auntie sign de form, den she ask, wa dat confirmation number mean?
We park by de visitor center and walk down to de praise house.
De nurse say blood pressure high, so Granny say she gwine eat less salt.
Mi cousin from Atlanta say y'all, but wen he come home he say oona.

Sentences: De court date been set fo Monday, June eighth, at ten o'clock.
Moon ain shame fo shine, and people ain shame fo talk.
Crab walk sideways, but e still reach de creek.
Don count de oyster befo de tide pull back.
Sweetgrass bend een de storm, but e ain break easy.
Empty pot mek de loudest clatter een de kitchen.
Wen de rooster crow too soon, day still wait fo sun.
Root deep, tree strong; family close, heart strong.
De fish dat laugh at de net soon learn rope.
A slow paddle kin cross a wide ribba.
Wen rice done cook, no need fo beat de pot.
Q: Weh de ferry leave from? A: E leave from de dock by de marina.
Q: Who bake de benne wafers? A: Aunt Liza bake um dis maanin.
Q: Wen de meeting start? A: E start at seven o'clock dis evenin.
Q: Why de road close? A: De crew da fix de bridge.
Q: How much rice we need? A: Two cup should be nuff fo supper.
Q: Is de praise house open today? A: Yes, e open after noon.
Q: Wa you tink bout de new teacher? A: Mi tink she patient and fair.
Q: Can oona help wid de boat? A: Yes, we kin come after lunch.
Q: Weh mi put de keys? A: You lef um on de blue shelf.
Q: Did de package arrive? A: No, de mail carrier ain bring um yet.
Close de do befo de sand blow een.
Tell Uncle Ben fo bring de net to de dock.

Turn de pot low and stir de okra slow.
Send de message wen you reach Beaufort.
Keep de baby away from de hot stove.
Read de sentence loud so everybody kin hear.
Wash de collard greens three time.
Check de tire pressure befo we cross de bridge.
Save de receipt fo de church treasurer.
